standard_description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations.
sources: NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Operations and Algebraic Thinking (OA) K–5, 2011
Burger, E. B. & Shaughnessy, M. (2012). Number Sense and Nonsense. Math Solutions.
Ashlock, R. B. (2010). Error Patterns in Computation (10th ed.). Pearson.
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.description: Student confuses the Commutative Property (a + b = b + a) with the Identity Property (a + 0 = a). When asked to identify the Commutative Property, student selects an Identity Property example (e.g., 7 + 0 = 7) because both involve 'something + something = answer'.
misconceptions.example: Selects '7 + 0 = 7' as Commutative Property instead of '4 + 5 = 5 + 4'.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions p.6 — 'Students may conflate the two addition properties when they only see symbolic examples without concrete/pictorial models.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use manipulatives: show two groups swapping positions (Commutative) vs. one group with an empty plate (Identity). Explicit verbal labels required.

misconceptions.description: Student incorrectly applies the odd/even sum rule. Common errors: (a) believes odd + even = even; (b) believes even + even = odd; (c) applies the rule inconsistently by checking only the tens digit rather than the ones digit.
misconceptions.example: For '3 + 4', student says the sum is even because 4 is even (ignores that 3 is odd).
misconceptions.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.22 — 'Children often over-generalize even/odd rules from multiplication to addition without testing.'
misconceptions.remediation: Count-by-2 visual: circle every other number on a number line. Build odd+odd, odd+even, even+even cases empirically before stating rules.

misconceptions.short_label: Row increment confused with row label
misconceptions.description: In the addition table, student reports that the row for 3 increases by 3 each time, rather than by 1. Student confuses the fixed addend (3) with the increment between consecutive entries (+1 per column).
misconceptions.example: Row 3: 3, 4, 5, 6... student says 'it goes up by 3'.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Lesson 1 Teacher Edition — 'A common error is that students report the row header number as the increment.'
misconceptions.remediation: Have student physically trace adjacent cells and compute difference. Ask: 'What changes as we move one column right?'

misconceptions.description: Student recognizes that diagonal entries in the addition table form a constant-sum pattern but reports the wrong diagonal or wrong value. Often selects the main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right) when asked about anti-diagonal (top-right to bottom-left), or vice versa.
misconceptions.example: When asked which diagonal shows equal sums, student points to the main diagonal instead of the anti-diagonal.
misconceptions.citation: Illustrative Mathematics Grade 3 Unit 1 — 'Students benefit from explicitly labeling diagonals before making claims about patterns.'
misconceptions.remediation: Color-code diagonals. Have student record the sum of each anti-diagonal before generalizing.

misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies the main diagonal of the addition table as the 'doubles' but over-generalizes: claims ALL items on this diagonal are even, without reasoning that odd + odd = even. Student may also claim only the doubles row/column produces evens.
misconceptions.example: Student says '3 + 3 = 6 is even because it's a double, but 3 + 5 can't be even.'
misconceptions.citation: Burger & Shaughnessy (2012) p.41 — 'Overgeneralizing doubles as the only source of even sums is a persistent misconception through G4.'
misconceptions.remediation: Present counterexamples: 2 + 4 = 6 (not a double but still even). Build even/odd classification from pairability, not just from doubles.

misconceptions.short_label: Skip-count increment confused with skip-count starting value
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies a skip-counting pattern but applies the rule starting from 0 rather than from the first given term, producing an off-by-one error in extending the sequence.
misconceptions.example: Sequence 4, 6, 8, 10 → student says next is 10 (restarts from 0+2) instead of 12.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions p.8 — 'Students must distinguish between the generator (rule) and the starting value of a sequence.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use a number line: mark the first term, then apply the rule by hopping forward. Emphasize 'where you start, then hop'.

misconceptions.description: Student notices that the addition table is symmetric about the main diagonal (because addition is commutative) but describes it incorrectly — e.g., claims the table is symmetric about the center point rather than the main diagonal, or claims entire rows equal entire columns.
misconceptions.example: Student says 'row 2 and column 2 have the same numbers' but means different positions within each.
misconceptions.citation: Smarter Balanced G3 Item Specs 2015 — 'Symmetry of addition table is a high-Bloom item; expect precision errors in how symmetry is described.'
misconceptions.remediation: Fold a printed addition table along the main diagonal. Discuss: which cells land on top of each other? Introduce vocabulary 'symmetric about the diagonal'.

misconceptions.short_label: Confusing a pattern observation with a rule
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies a specific example of a pattern (e.g., 3 + 4 = 7) but cannot generalize to a rule (e.g., odd + even = odd). Student treats each pattern observation as isolated rather than as evidence of a general property.
misconceptions.example: Student checks that 3+4=7 is odd but cannot predict whether 7+8 will be odd or even without computing.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions p.9 — 'Moving from noticing a pattern to articulating a generalized rule requires explicit instructional scaffolding.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use 'If...then...' sentence frames: 'If I add an odd number and an even number, then the sum is always ___.' Test with 5 examples, then state the rule.
